content.text:
Honest answer first: you've built a beautiful study system but you haven't actually studied yet. The notes existing in the vault is not the same as the knowledge being in your head. With a mock exam in hand and "pretty much nothing" learned, the right move is **stop generating, start retrieving**.

Here's the plan.

## Step 0 — Diagnose, don't study (today, 1–2 hours)

Before any studying, take the mock exam **closed-book**. Set a timer for the real exam length, no notes, no Claude. You will fail. That's the point — you need to know:

1. What format does the exam use? Multiple choice, short answer, derivations, code, diagrams?
2. Which topics get the most weight?
3. Which questions could you almost answer vs. which made no sense at all?
4. How much time pressure is there?

Without this, you'll study the wrong things. A mock exam is a map. Use it as one before you mine it for content.

After the attempt, score yourself ruthlessly. Then in Agent Client:

> Read `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/mock-exams/mock-exam.pdf`. For every question on the exam:
>
> 1. Identify which lecture(s) it tests (cross-reference with `10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/`)
> 2. Identify which atomic concepts in `20-Concepts/` are required to answer it
> 3. Classify each question by type: definition, application, derivation, comparison, computation
> 4. Estimate difficulty 1–5
>
> Output a table: question # | topic | lecture(s) | concepts needed | type | difficulty | exam weight (if marks are shown).
>
> Then produce a "topic priority" ranking: which lectures are most heavily tested, which are barely tested, which concepts appear in multiple questions. Save the analysis to `40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/exam-blueprint.md`.

That blueprint tells you where to spend your time. Not all 11 lectures are equal — the exam decides what's important, not the syllabus.

## Step 1 — Build a personal "what I don't know" list (day 1 evening)

Cross-reference your mock exam attempt against the blueprint:

- Topics where you got the question wrong AND the topic is heavily weighted → **priority 1**, study first
- Topics where you got it right but felt unsure → **priority 2**, review
- Topics you nailed cold → **priority 3**, occasional review only

Tell Claude:

> Based on `40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/exam-blueprint.md` and the topics I'll list below, generate a study plan covering N days until my exam. Each day should mix:
>
> - 60% active recall: Spaced Repetition reviews + practice problems
> - 30% new learning: reading concept notes I haven't internalized
> - 10% synthesis: writing out one topic from memory
>
> Front-load priority 1 topics. Save to `40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/study-plan.md` as a checklist with date headers.
>
> Topics by priority:
> - P1: [list from your wrong-answer review]
> - P2: [list from "unsure" answers]
> - P3: [list from confident answers]

## Step 2 — Daily loop (the actual studying)

This is the part nobody wants to hear: **studying is recall, not reading.** Re-reading your beautiful notes feels productive but barely moves the needle. The research on this is unambiguous — testing yourself beats re-reading by a wide margin.

Daily, in this order:

**1. Spaced Repetition reviews first (15–25 min).** Click the brain icon. Review every card due that day. This is the floor — non-negotiable. If you do nothing else, do this.

**2. Active recall on the day's priority topic (30–45 min).**

For each topic:
- Close all notes
- Get a blank page (paper or blank Obsidian note)
- Write everything you know about the topic from memory: definitions, equations, diagrams, when to use it, how it relates to other concepts, common pitfalls
- Set a timer for 10 minutes per topic to prevent rabbit-holing
- Then open your concept notes and compare. Mark every gap.

This sounds tedious. It's the part that actually works. Reading the note feels like learning, but you only know if you actually know it when you try to produce it from nothing.

**3. Practice problems (20–40 min).**

Ask Claude:

> Generate 3 exam-style problems on [topic]. Match the difficulty and format of `mock-exam.pdf`. Don't show me the answers. After I attempt them, I'll paste my answer and you'll grade it harshly — point out every error, missing step, and lazy reasoning.

Do them on paper, then paste your attempt back and let Claude grade. The grading is where the learning happens. Don't argue with the grade — if Claude says you missed a step, you missed a step.

**4. Process the gaps (10 min).**

For every error or gap from steps 2 and 3, decide:
- Was this a knowledge gap? → make a new flashcard, link to the relevant concept
- Was this a careless mistake? → write the lesson in a "lessons.md" file (e.g. "always check if convolution is associative before applying property X")
- Was this a misunderstanding of the question? → flag to revisit

Tell Claude:

> Add the following to my flashcard files in the right lectures: [paste gaps]. Use the multi-line `?` format. Tag with `flashcards` and `Computer-Vision`.

Now those gaps come back through Spaced Repetition until they stick.

## Step 3 — Weekly: synthesis and re-test

**Once a week (or more often near the exam):**

- Re-take a portion of the mock exam closed-book. Same one — you should see your own progress over time. If something you got wrong last week is still wrong, that's a flashing red light.
- Pick one heavy topic (say, CNNs). Open a blank note. Without looking at anything, write a 1-page explainer of the topic as if teaching a peer who took intro programming but no ML. Diagrams included. Then compare to your notes.
- Generate one new "stretch" problem per priority-1 topic and attempt it.

The teach-it-back exercise is brutal and revealing. If you can't explain it cleanly, you don't know it — even if you can recognize the right answer when you see it.

## What NOT to do

- **Don't generate more notes.** You have 11 lectures of beautiful notes. Generating more is procrastination dressed as productivity.
- **Don't redo the ingest with better diagrams.** Diagrams are tools, not knowledge. The pretty Mermaid won't help you on the exam.
- **Don't re-read passively.** If you find yourself opening a concept note and just reading it, stop. Close it, write what you know, then open it to check.
- **Don't optimize the study plan instead of executing it.** The plan you make in 30 minutes that you actually follow beats the perfect plan you spend 3 days building.
- **Don't trust the flashcards too much.** Flashcards train recognition and atomic recall. They don't train derivations, problem-solving, or synthesis. The mock-exam-style practice is what builds those — flashcards are the foundation, not the whole house.

## A concrete starting prompt

Right now, while it's fresh:

> Read `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/mock-exams/mock-exam.pdf` and produce two outputs:
>
> 1. `40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/exam-blueprint.md` — table mapping every question to lecture(s), concepts, type, and difficulty as described above
> 2. `40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/diagnostic.md` — a closed-book version of the exam I can attempt now. Include the original questions but stripped of any answer hints, marking schemes, or worked examples. Add a "my answer" blank under each.
>
> Do NOT show me the answers anywhere. After I complete the diagnostic, I'll come back and you'll mark it against the original mock exam.

Run the blueprint, attempt the diagnostic on paper today, and come back with how badly it went. That's where studying actually starts. We'll plan the daily loop based on what the diagnostic reveals — there's no point planning around lectures you've already half-internalized vs. lectures you've never opened.

How long until the real exam?
Model set. Ready when you are — let me know what you'd like to do next.

Read 30-Sources/Computer-Vision/mock-exams/mock-exam.pdf. Produce 40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/exam-blueprint.md with:

Table: question # | marks/weight | topic | lecture(s) | concepts needed (link to 20-Concepts/) | type (definition/application/derivation/computation/comparison) | estimated difficulty 1–5
Topic frequency ranking — which topics carry the most marks
"Bang-for-buck" ranking — topics with high marks AND high difficulty are the priority; topics with low marks AND high difficulty are skip candidates
List of concept files in 20-Concepts/ that are required and don't yet exist (gaps)

Also produce 40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/diagnostic.md: the exam questions stripped of any hints/answers, with a "my answer" blank under each. Don't show answers anywhere.

## Topic frequency — by total marks

| Rank | Topic cluster | Total marks | # of questions | Lectures |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fourier & sampling** (FT 2D, Nyquist, FFT, downsampling) | **1.95** | 5 (Q2, 7, 11, 16, 25) | 10 |
| 2 | **Transformers & attention** (positional encoding, MHA, self-attention) | **1.35** | 3 (Q1, 14, 26) | 17 |
| 3 | **Hough transform** (line detection) | **1.30** | 2 (Q3, 20) | 12 |
| 4 | **PyTorch** (autograd, broadcasting, tensor ops) | **1.20** | 4 (Q19, 24, 27, 28) | 14 |
| 5 | **CNN basics** (pooling, receptive field, dimensions) | **0.90** | 3 (Q15, 17, 23) | 16 |
| 6 | **Edge detection & gradients** (Sobel, Canny NMS, gradient direction) | **0.90** | 3 (Q13, 18, 21) | 11 |
| 7 | **Segmentation / detection** (IoU, Dice loss) | **0.60** | 2 (Q4, 8) | 18 |
| 8 | **Corner / keypoint detection** (structure tensor, SIFT) | **0.60** | 2 (Q6, 10) | 11–12 |
| 9 | **CNN architectures** (Inception) | **0.30** | 1 (Q5) | 17 |
| 10 | **ICP** (point cloud alignment) | **0.30** | 1 (Q9) | 12 |
| 11 | **Binary objects / shape descriptors** (solidity) | **0.30** | 1 (Q22) | 13 |
| 12 | **Self-supervised / CLIP** | **0.30** | 1 (Q12) | 19 |

**Coverage observation:** Lecture 10 (Fourier) and Lecture 17 (Transformers/Attention) together = 3.30 pts = 33% of the exam. Lectures 15 (intro to DL), 20 (autoencoders) are **not tested at all** in this mock — do not over-invest there for this exam style.

## Bang-for-buck ranking

Scored as `marks × difficulty` weighting, then grouped:

### Priority 1 — high marks **and** high difficulty (study first)

| Topic | Marks | Avg difficulty | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fourier & sampling** | 1.95 | 2.8 | Five questions, one of them open-ended (Q2). Q7 (stripe spectrum) and Q25 (Nyquist applied) are conceptually tricky. The single highest-leverage topic. |
| **Transformers & attention** | 1.35 | 3.3 | Q1 is open-ended (positional encoding + 2 schemes), Q26 is a 3×3 matrix computation. If you can't do the matrix math under time pressure you lose 0.6 pts that should be free. |
| **Hough transform** | 1.30 | 2.5 | Q3 alone is **1.0 point** — the largest single question on the exam. Q20 is also Hough. Mastering Hough conceptually + algorithmically is the best ROI of any topic. |

### Priority 2 — mid marks, moderate difficulty

| Topic | Marks | Avg difficulty | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PyTorch** (broadcasting + tensor ops) | 1.20 | 2.5 | Q24 and Q28 are broadcasting traps. Drill them on a notebook until automatic. Q19, Q27 are gimmes if you've used the API. |
| **Edge detection / gradients** | 0.90 | 2.0 | Q18 (edge orientation perpendicular to gradient) is a classic trap. Sobel/Canny are largely recall. |
| **Corner / keypoint detection** | 0.60 | 2.5 | Q6 forces you to memorise the 3 eigenvalue cases (corner: both large; edge: one large; flat: both small). One concrete fact pattern → easy 0.3. |

### Priority 3 — secure these (low difficulty, don't lose them)

| Topic | Marks | Avg difficulty | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CNN basics** | 0.90 | 1.7 | Pooling motivation, receptive field, dimension math — should be free. Q23 just needs `floor((H − k)/s + 1)`. |
| **Segmentation / detection** | 0.60 | 1.5 | IoU=0 means no overlap; Dice loss is "the imbalance loss". Two recall facts. |
| **CLIP / contrastive** | 0.30 | 2.0 | One question, one fact: contrastive image-text alignment. |
| **Inception** | 0.30 | 2.0 | One question, one fact: parallel branches. |
| **ICP** | 0.30 | 2.0 | One fact: aligns point clouds when correspondences are unknown. |

### Skip candidates — low marks, high difficulty (don't sink hours here)

| Topic | Marks | Difficulty | Why skip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solidity / shape descriptors** (Q22) | 0.30 | 3 | Single question, requires careful reasoning about convex hull ratios, easy to confuse with elongation/eccentricity. If you don't already know it, answer with intuition (low solidity = concavities) and move on. Don't memorise the full shape-descriptor zoo for 0.3 pts. |

## Concept gaps in 20-Concepts/

Going through every required concept above against the existing `20-Concepts/` directory: **the vault is well-stocked.** All 28 questions have at least one matching existing concept note.

A few are *adjacent but not isolated atomically*; consider whether to split, but only if you spot a recall failure during practice:

- **FFT (asymptotic complexity)** — Q11. Currently subsumed in [[../../20-Concepts/fourier-transform-2d]]. If your note doesn't already say "O(N log N) vs O(N²)" verbatim, either add that line to `fourier-transform-2d.md` or split out a tiny `fast-fourier-transform.md`. Latter is cleaner.
- **Sobel operator** — Q13. Lives inside [[../../20-Concepts/gradient-edge-kernels]]. Probably fine; Sobel isn't atomic enough to deserve its own note unless you confuse it with Prewitt / Scharr.
- **Solidity (specific descriptor)** — Q22. Lives in [[../../20-Concepts/shape-descriptors]]. The card-level recall ("solidity = area / convex-hull area; low → concavities/branches") is the bit that matters; don't split unless the lumped note is too long to scan.
- **NMS in Canny vs object-detection NMS** — Q21. You have separate [[../../20-Concepts/canny-edge-detector]] and [[../../20-Concepts/non-maximum-suppression]]. Make sure the Canny note explicitly contains the "thinning to single-pixel-wide edges" definition — that's the answer here, not the bbox-suppression version.

**True missing concepts:** none. The structural gap is recall, not coverage.

## Next steps

1. Take [[diagnostic|the diagnostic]] closed-book on paper. Time it: 90 min.
2. Mark it against the original PDF (you, not me — grading yourself is part of learning).
3. Come back with: question-by-question right/wrong/unsure, and we'll re-rank priorities based on **your** weak spots, not the average difficulty above.

## Topic frequency — by total marks

| Rank | Topic cluster | Total marks | # of questions | Lectures |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fourier & sampling** (FT 2D, Nyquist, FFT, downsampling) | **1.95** | 5 (Q2, 7, 11, 16, 25) | 10 |
| 2 | **Transformers & attention** (positional encoding, MHA, self-attention) | **1.35** | 3 (Q1, 14, 26) | 17 |
| 3 | **Hough transform** (line detection) | **1.30** | 2 (Q3, 20) | 12 |
| 4 | **PyTorch** (autograd, broadcasting, tensor ops) | **1.20** | 4 (Q19, 24, 27, 28) | 14 |
| 5 | **CNN basics** (pooling, receptive field, dimensions) | **0.90** | 3 (Q15, 17, 23) | 16 |
| 6 | **Edge detection & gradients** (Sobel, Canny NMS, gradient direction) | **0.90** | 3 (Q13, 18, 21) | 11 |
| 7 | **Segmentation / detection** (IoU, Dice loss) | **0.60** | 2 (Q4, 8) | 18 |
| 8 | **Corner / keypoint detection** (structure tensor, SIFT) | **0.60** | 2 (Q6, 10) | 11–12 |
| 9 | **CNN architectures** (Inception) | **0.30** | 1 (Q5) | 17 |
| 10 | **ICP** (point cloud alignment) | **0.30** | 1 (Q9) | 12 |
| 11 | **Binary objects / shape descriptors** (solidity) | **0.30** | 1 (Q22) | 13 |
| 12 | **Self-supervised / CLIP** | **0.30** | 1 (Q12) | 19 |

**Coverage observation:** Lecture 10 (Fourier) and Lecture 17 (Transformers/Attention) together = 3.30 pts = 33% of the exam. Lectures 15 (intro to DL), 20 (autoencoders) are **not tested at all** in this mock — do not over-invest there for this exam style.

## Bang-for-buck ranking

Scored as `marks × difficulty` weighting, then grouped:

### Priority 1 — high marks **and** high difficulty (study first)

| Topic | Marks | Avg difficulty | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fourier & sampling** | 1.95 | 2.8 | Five questions, one of them open-ended (Q2). Q7 (stripe spectrum) and Q25 (Nyquist applied) are conceptually tricky. The single highest-leverage topic. |
| **Transformers & attention** | 1.35 | 3.3 | Q1 is open-ended (positional encoding + 2 schemes), Q26 is a 3×3 matrix computation. If you can't do the matrix math under time pressure you lose 0.6 pts that should be free. |
| **Hough transform** | 1.30 | 2.5 | Q3 alone is **1.0 point** — the largest single question on the exam. Q20 is also Hough. Mastering Hough conceptually + algorithmically is the best ROI of any topic. |

### Priority 2 — mid marks, moderate difficulty

| Topic | Marks | Avg difficulty | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PyTorch** (broadcasting + tensor ops) | 1.20 | 2.5 | Q24 and Q28 are broadcasting traps. Drill them on a notebook until automatic. Q19, Q27 are gimmes if you've used the API. |
| **Edge detection / gradients** | 0.90 | 2.0 | Q18 (edge orientation perpendicular to gradient) is a classic trap. Sobel/Canny are largely recall. |
| **Corner / keypoint detection** | 0.60 | 2.5 | Q6 forces you to memorise the 3 eigenvalue cases (corner: both large; edge: one large; flat: both small). One concrete fact pattern → easy 0.3. |

### Priority 3 — secure these (low difficulty, don't lose them)

| Topic | Marks | Avg difficulty | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CNN basics** | 0.90 | 1.7 | Pooling motivation, receptive field, dimension math — should be free. Q23 just needs `floor((H − k)/s + 1)`. |
| **Segmentation / detection** | 0.60 | 1.5 | IoU=0 means no overlap; Dice loss is "the imbalance loss". Two recall facts. |
| **CLIP / contrastive** | 0.30 | 2.0 | One question, one fact: contrastive image-text alignment. |
| **Inception** | 0.30 | 2.0 | One question, one fact: parallel branches. |
| **ICP** | 0.30 | 2.0 | One fact: aligns point clouds when correspondences are unknown. |

### Skip candidates — low marks, high difficulty (don't sink hours here)

| Topic | Marks | Difficulty | Why skip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solidity / shape descriptors** (Q22) | 0.30 | 3 | Single question, requires careful reasoning about convex hull ratios, easy to confuse with elongation/eccentricity. If you don't already know it, answer with intuition (low solidity = concavities) and move on. Don't memorise the full shape-descriptor zoo for 0.3 pts. |

## Concept gaps in 20-Concepts/

Going through every required concept above against the existing `20-Concepts/` directory: **the vault is well-stocked.** All 28 questions have at least one matching existing concept note.

A few are *adjacent but not isolated atomically*; consider whether to split, but only if you spot a recall failure during practice:

- **FFT (asymptotic complexity)** — Q11. Currently subsumed in [[../../20-Concepts/fourier-transform-2d]]. If your note doesn't already say "O(N log N) vs O(N²)" verbatim, either add that line to `fourier-transform-2d.md` or split out a tiny `fast-fourier-transform.md`. Latter is cleaner.
- **Sobel operator** — Q13. Lives inside [[../../20-Concepts/gradient-edge-kernels]]. Probably fine; Sobel isn't atomic enough to deserve its own note unless you confuse it with Prewitt / Scharr.
- **Solidity (specific descriptor)** — Q22. Lives in [[../../20-Concepts/shape-descriptors]]. The card-level recall ("solidity = area / convex-hull area; low → concavities/branches") is the bit that matters; don't split unless the lumped note is too long to scan.
- **NMS in Canny vs object-detection NMS** — Q21. You have separate [[../../20-Concepts/canny-edge-detector]] and [[../../20-Concepts/non-maximum-suppression]]. Make sure the Canny note explicitly contains the "thinning to single-pixel-wide edges" definition — that's the answer here, not the bbox-suppression version.

**True missing concepts:** none. The structural gap is recall, not coverage.

## Next steps

1. Take [[diagnostic|the diagnostic]] closed-book on paper. Time it: 90 min.
2. Mark it against the original PDF (you, not me — grading yourself is part of learning).
3. Come back with: question-by-question right/wrong/unsure, and we'll re-rank priorities based on **your** weak spots, not the average difficulty above.

# Computer Vision — Closed-Book Diagnostic

> **Rules.** 1h 30m timer. Pen and paper (or just type into the "My answer" blanks below). No notes, no Claude, no Google. If you don't know, write "blank" or your best guess. Do **not** scroll back to the blueprint or open `mock-exam.pdf` until you're done.
>
> When the timer ends, mark yourself against the original PDF (in `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/mock-exams/mock-exam.pdf`). Be honest — guesses that happened to be right still count as "unsure" for study planning purposes.

---

## Conceptual Open-Ended Questions

### Question 1 (0.75 pts)

Explain why positional encoding is required in transformers, and discuss briefly two different ways of encoding positional information.

**My answer:**



---

### Question 2 (0.75 pts)

Explain, conceptually, how you would use the Fourier transform to downsample an image by a factor of two. Explain also how you can blur it.

**My answer:**



---

## Algorithmic Open-Ended Question

### Question 3 (1.0 pts)

Explain what the Hough transform is and provide a conceptual description of its implementation for line detection.

**My answer:**



---

## Multiple Choice Questions (0.3 pts each)

### Question 4

In the context of the IoU metric, what does an IoU of 0.0 indicate?

- A. Zero overlap between prediction and ground truth
- B. Perfect overlap between prediction and ground truth
- C. The ground truth is a subset of the prediction
- D. The prediction is a subset of the ground truth

**My answer:**

---

### Question 5

What is the defining characteristic of the Inception (GoogLeNet) module?

- A. Dense connectivity between all layers
- B. Extremely deep stacks of 3×3 convolutions
- C. Parallel convolutional branches
- D. Global self-attention across all patches

**My answer:**

---

### Question 6

Given the second moment matrix (structure tensor) of an image patch, how do the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ relate to the region's characteristics?

- A. If both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are large, the patch contains a corner
- B. If both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are small, the patch contains a dominant edge
- C. If both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are large, the patch is perfectly flat
- D. If both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are small, the patch contains a corner

**My answer:**

---

### Question 7

If an image contains a series of horizontal stripes, where would you expect to see high-magnitude coefficients in its shifted Fourier spectrum?

- A. At the four corners of the spectrum
- B. Uniformly distributed across the center region
- C. Along the horizontal axis passing through the center
- D. Along the vertical axis passing through the center

**My answer:**

---

### Question 8

Which loss function is specifically effective for semantic segmentation tasks with extreme class imbalance?

- A. Mean Squared Error (MSE) Loss
- B. Huber (Robust) Loss
- C. Dice Loss (or IoU-based loss)
- D. Standard Cross-Entropy Loss

**My answer:**

---

### Question 9

What is the primary function of the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm?

- A. Aligning point clouds when initial correspondences are unknown
- B. Aligning point clouds when initial correspondences are known
- C. Detecting blobs in point clouds at multiple spatial scales
- D. Performing non-rigid warping of point clouds to fit a model

**My answer:**

---

### Question 10

Which of the following property is a key advantage of the SIFT descriptor over traditional template matching?

- A. Independence from local intensity gradients
- B. Linear computational complexity with image area
- C. Robustness to local minima in feature matching
- D. Robustness to changes in scale and rotation

**My answer:**

---

### Question 11

For an input of size $N$, how does the execution time of the FFT scale relative to the standard DFT?

- A. FFT scales as $O(N^2)$, providing no asymptotic improvement over DFT
- B. FFT scales as $O(N \log N)$, but is slower than DFT due to recursive overhead
- C. FFT scales as $O(N \log N)$, making it significantly faster for large $N$
- D. FFT scales as $O(N)$, providing linear scaling for all $N$

**My answer:**

---

### Question 12

What is the main training objective of CLIP?

- A. Learn a joint embedding space where matching image–text pairs have high similarity and mismatched pairs have low similarity
- B. Optimize a multi-class classification objective over image–text pairs using fixed category labels derived from captions
- C. Minimize reconstruction error between images and their corresponding text descriptions in a shared latent space
- D. Maximize similarity between image and text embeddings via data augmentation

**My answer:**

---

### Question 13

What is the primary function of the Sobel operator in image processing?

- A. Applying a low-pass filter to remove salt-and-pepper noise
- B. Approximating the second-order image derivative for blob detection
- C. Approximating the first-order image gradient (magnitude and direction)
- D. Applying a non-linear rank filter for edge preservation

**My answer:**

---

### Question 14

What is the primary motivation for using "Multi-Head" attention?

- A. Enabling the model to process sequences of variable length
- B. Stacking multiple Transformer encoders for greater depth
- C. Attending to information from different representation subspaces
- D. Processing multiple tokens simultaneously in a single batch

**My answer:**

---

### Question 15

What is the primary motivation for including pooling layers in a CNN architecture?

- A. Introduce spatial invariance and reduce computational load
- B. Introduce channel invariance and increase computational load
- C. Introduce channel invariance and reduce computational load
- D. Introduce spatial invariance and increase perceptual information

**My answer:**

---

### Question 16

What happens if the Nyquist sampling theorem is violated (i.e., the sampling rate is too low)?

- A. Low-frequency components are amplified, causing saturation
- B. High-frequency components are completely removed from the signal
- C. High-frequency components are folded into lower frequencies (aliasing)
- D. Low-frequency components are folded into higher frequencies

**My answer:**

---

### Question 17

Which of the following best defines the receptive field of a neuron in a hidden layer of a CNN?

- A. Region in the output map that is influenced by that neuron's activation
- B. Total number of weights in the filter associated with that neuron
- C. Total number of neurons in the previous layer that connect to it
- D. Region in the input image that can influence that neuron's activation

**My answer:**

---

### Question 18

In the context of the image gradient $\nabla f = [g_x, g_y]^T$, how is the edge orientation (the direction along the edge itself) typically defined?

- A. Perpendicular to the second-moment vector: $\arctan(g_x / g_y) \pm 90°$
- B. Parallel to the gradient vector: $\arctan(g_y / g_x)$
- C. Perpendicular to the gradient vector: $\arctan(g_y / g_x) \pm 90°$
- D. Parallel to the second-moment vector: $\arctan(g_x / g_y)$

**My answer:**

---

### Question 19

What is the primary effect of `with torch.no_grad():` during the evaluation phase of a model?

- A. Disables gradient tracking and reduces memory usage
- B. Disables weight updates and speeds up training
- C. Enables gradient tracking but disables weight updates
- D. Enables weight updates but disables gradient tracking

**My answer:**

---

### Question 20

Which of the following algorithms can be used to detect straight lines?

- A. The Sobel filter
- B. The Hough Transform
- C. The Canny filter
- D. The Laplacian operator for zero-crossing detection

**My answer:**

---

### Question 21

What is the primary purpose of "Non-Maximum Suppression" (NMS) in the Canny edge detection algorithm?

- A. To remove edges with low gradient magnitude
- B. To connect fragmented edge segments into closed contours
- C. To thin the edges to a single pixel width
- D. To thicken the edges to make them more visible

**My answer:**

---

### Question 22

An object with a low ratio of its area to its convex hull area (solidity) most likely exhibits:

- A. A perfectly convex and smooth boundary
- B. An elongated shape
- C. Significant concavities or a branched structure
- D. An area significantly larger than its perimeter

**My answer:**

---

### Question 23

What is the effect of a 2×2 max pooling layer with stride 2 on the spatial dimensions of a feature map?

- A. Height and width are both reduced by 25%
- B. Height and width are both reduced by 50%
- C. Height and width are both increased by 25%
- D. Height and width are both increased by 50%

**My answer:**

---

### Question 24

Consider tensors $X$ with shape $(4, 1, 16)$ and $Y$ with shape $(8, 1)$. What will be the shape of the result of $X \cdot Y$ (elementwise multiplication) in PyTorch?

- A. RuntimeError
- B. (8, 4, 16)
- C. (4, 8, 16)
- D. (4, 1, 16)

**My answer:**

---

### Question 25

If you want to resolve features as small as 1 cm in an image, what should be the minimum sampling rate (effective pixel resolution) required to satisfy the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem?

- A. No more than 2 pixels per cm
- B. At least 0.5 pixels per cm
- C. No more than 0.5 pixels per cm
- D. At least 2 pixels per cm

**My answer:**

---

### Question 26

We consider a self-attention mechanism where the attention weights have already been computed.

The attention matrix is

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4 & 0.1 \\ 0.3 & 0.2 & 0.5 \\ 0.2 & 0.1 & 0.7 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$A_{ij}$ represents the attention that token $i$ should be paying to token $j$.

The value matrix is

$$
V = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 10 \\ 10 & 0 \\ 20 & 20 \end{pmatrix}
$$

where rows are the tokens and columns represent the dimensionality of the token vectors.

Which is the resulting representation of $x_3$?

- A. (14, 15)
- B. (16, 15)
- C. (15, 16)
- D. (13, 16)

**My answer:**

---

### Question 27

Suppose you have a tensor $X$ with shape $(4, 8, 16)$. What is the shape of the output of `X.sum(dim=1)`?

- A. (4, 16)
- B. (8, 16)
- C. (4, 1, 16)
- D. (1, 8, 16)

**My answer:**

---

### Question 28

What will be the output of the following code?

```python
x = torch.ones(2, 2)
y = torch.zeros(2, 1)
z = x + y
print(z.shape)
```

- A. RuntimeError
- B. torch.Size([2, 1])
- C. torch.Size([4, 2])
- D. torch.Size([2, 2])

**My answer:**

---

## After you finish

1. Open `30-Sources/Computer-Vision/mock-exams/mock-exam.pdf` (no — there's no answer key in it; it's the same exam).
2. For MCQs, you'll need to grade against your own knowledge or come back to me with your answers and I'll mark them.
3. For each question, classify yourself: **right & confident** / **right but unsure** / **wrong** / **didn't know**.
4. Bring that classification back. We'll redo the priority ranking based on **your** gaps, not the population average.

## 1. The problem it solves

Imagine you have a grainy aerial photo of a parking lot and you want a computer to find the white painted lines that mark the parking spaces. After running an edge detector you get a binary image — pixels that are "edges" vs "not edges." But "edge pixel" is local: each pixel just knows it's a boundary, it doesn't know it's part of *that* long line three rows up. The lines are also noisy: pixels are missing where paint flaked off, and there are spurious edge pixels from leaves and shadows. You need an algorithm that takes a chaotic cloud of edge pixels and says: "there are lines here, here, and here, even though no single line is unbroken." Hough transform is the algorithm that does this.

## 2. The naive approach and why it fails

The obvious idea: slide a "line template" over the image and look for matches, like template matching for a face. This breaks for several reasons.

- Lines come in **infinitely many orientations and positions** — you'd need infinitely many templates.
- A line is a 1-pixel-wide structure; correlation with a template is dominated by the surrounding zeros and gives a flat, uninformative response.
- Lines can be **broken** (gaps in the paint). A template requires the line to be continuous; even one missing pixel kills the match.
- You don't know the line's **length** in advance. A template assumes a fixed length; real lines are arbitrary.

Pattern matching looks at *what's there* in image space. Hough does something fundamentally different.

## 3. The key insight

Each edge pixel could belong to many possible lines (any line passing through it). Instead of asking "is *this pixel* on a line?", ask every edge pixel to **vote for every line it could possibly be on**. The line that gets the most votes wins — because only a real line in the image will collect votes from many pixels at once.

## 4. Tiny worked example — 5×5 image, 3 edge pixels

Image coordinates: $(x, y)$ where $x$ is column (0 left → 4 right), $y$ is row (0 top → 4 bottom). Edge pixels at **(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)** — they lie on the diagonal line $y = x$.

We use the parametrization $\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$ (explained in section 5). For tractability, I'll discretize $\theta$ to just **four bins**: $0°, 45°, 90°, 135°$. And $\rho$ to integer bins from $-6$ to $6$ (image diagonal is $\approx 5.66$).

The accumulator starts empty (all zeros).

### Pixel (1,1) votes

For each $\theta$, compute $\rho$:

| θ | cos θ | sin θ | ρ = 1·cos θ + 1·sin θ | ρ bin |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0° | 1 | 0 | 1.00 | 1 |
| 45° | 0.71 | 0.71 | 1.41 | 1 |
| 90° | 0 | 1 | 1.00 | 1 |
| 135° | −0.71 | 0.71 | 0.00 | 0 |

Increment those four cells. Accumulator after pixel (1,1):

|         | ρ=0 | ρ=1 | ρ=2 | ρ=3 | ρ=4 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|
| θ=0°    | 0 | **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| θ=45°   | 0 | **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| θ=90°   | 0 | **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| θ=135°  | **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

### Pixel (2,2) votes

| θ | ρ = 2·cos θ + 2·sin θ | ρ bin |
|---|---|---|
| 0° | 2.00 | 2 |
| 45° | 2.83 | 3 |
| 90° | 2.00 | 2 |
| 135° | 0.00 | 0 |

Accumulator after pixel (2,2):

|         | ρ=0 | ρ=1 | ρ=2 | ρ=3 | ρ=4 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|
| θ=0°    | 0 | 1 | **1** | 0 | 0 |
| θ=45°   | 0 | 1 | 0 | **1** | 0 |
| θ=90°   | 0 | 1 | **1** | 0 | 0 |
| θ=135°  | **2** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

Notice the (135°, 0) cell already has **2** votes — the same cell got hit twice. That's the magic happening.

### Pixel (3,3) votes

| θ | ρ = 3·cos θ + 3·sin θ | ρ bin |
|---|---|---|
| 0° | 3.00 | 3 |
| 45° | 4.24 | 4 |
| 90° | 3.00 | 3 |
| 135° | 0.00 | 0 |

Final accumulator:

|         | ρ=0 | ρ=1 | ρ=2 | ρ=3 | ρ=4 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|
| θ=0°    | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| θ=45°   | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| θ=90°   | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| θ=135°  | **3** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

### The peak

Peak: $(\theta = 135°, \rho = 0)$ with **3 votes**.

Decode: the detected line has normal at $135°$ from the x-axis, and is at distance $0$ from the origin. That's the line $y = x$ — exactly the line our three edge pixels lie on. The other cells show 1s scattered around (every pixel votes once at every $\theta$), but only the **true line's cell got every pixel to agree**, because only on that line do all three pixels share the same $(\theta, \rho)$.

That's the entire algorithm. The accumulator turns "find a structure across many pixels" into "find a peak in a 2D array."

## 5. Why ρ-θ instead of m-c

Tempting to parametrize a line as $y = mx + c$ and use $(m, c)$ as your accumulator axes. Failure: a vertical line has slope $m = \infty$. You can't discretize an infinite axis into bins. Even a *near*-vertical line gets a huge $m$, so your accumulator either has wildly non-uniform resolution or simply can't represent vertical lines. The fix: use the **normal form** $\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$, where $\theta \in [0°, 180°)$ is the angle of the line's *normal vector* and $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from origin to the line. Both axes are bounded ($\theta$ in a half-circle, $\rho$ in $[-D, D]$ where $D$ is the image diagonal), so the accumulator is finite-sized regardless of line orientation.

## 6. The full algorithm (lines)

1. **Edge-detect** the input image (typically [[Canny]]). You now have a binary edge map.
2. **Choose discretization**: pick step sizes $\Delta\theta$ (e.g. $1°$) and $\Delta\rho$ (e.g. 1 pixel). Allocate the 2D accumulator array of zeros, with rows indexed by $\theta$ bins, columns by $\rho$ bins.
3. **Vote**: for every edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$, loop over every $\theta$ in the grid, compute $\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$, round $\rho$ to its bin, and increment `accumulator[θ_bin, ρ_bin]` by 1.
4. **Find peaks**: scan the accumulator for cells whose count exceeds a threshold. Apply non-maximum suppression so that a thick clump of high-vote cells only yields one detected line each.
5. **Decode** each peak: a peak at $(\theta^*, \rho^*)$ corresponds to the line $\rho^* = x \cos\theta^* + y \sin\theta^*$ in image space.

## 7. Variants — circle detection

A circle has **three parameters**: centre $(a, b)$ and radius $r$. The implicit equation is $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$.

- **Known radius**: parameter space is 2D ($a, b$). Each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ votes for all $(a, b)$ such that $(x_i - a)^2 + (y_i - b)^2 = r^2$ — i.e. it votes along a **circle of radius $r$ centred at $(x_i, y_i)$** in parameter space. Edge pixels lying on the same image circle all vote through that circle's centre, producing a peak at $(a^*, b^*)$.
- **Unknown radius**: parameter space is 3D ($a, b, r$). Each edge pixel votes on a **cone** in this space (one circle per candidate $r$). Peaks at $(a^*, b^*, r^*)$.

**The cost.** Adding a parameter multiplies memory and compute by the number of bins along that axis. Lines: 2D accumulator, $O(N_\text{edges} \cdot N_\theta)$ votes. Circles with known $r$: same. Circles with unknown $r$: 3D accumulator, $O(N_\text{edges} \cdot N_\theta \cdot N_r)$ votes — typically 50–100× slower. Generalized Hough (arbitrary shapes) is even worse and is rarely used in practice for that reason.

## 8. The four facts you need for the exam

- **Core idea:** swap image space for parameter space; let edge pixels vote; peaks in the accumulator = detected shapes. Robust to noise and gaps because each pixel votes independently.
- **Use $\rho$-$\theta$ parametrization, not $m$-$c$:** $m$ blows up for vertical lines and can't be discretized; $\rho \in [-D, D]$ and $\theta \in [0°, 180°)$ are both bounded.
- **A single edge pixel becomes a sinusoid in $(\theta, \rho)$ space.** Multiple sinusoids that intersect at a common $(\theta^*, \rho^*)$ correspond to collinear pixels in the image — that intersection is the peak.
- **Pre-step is edge detection** (Canny). Post-step is non-maximum suppression on the accumulator. Cost grows multiplicatively with each parameter you add, so circle detection with unknown radius (3D accumulator) is much more expensive than line detection.

# Fourier & sampling — from scratch

## 1. The problem it solves

You have a noisy photo and you want to **blur** it (smooth out grain), or **resize** it (downsample for a thumbnail), or **detect what kind of texture is in it** (stripes? checkerboard? smooth gradient?). You could do all of these by sliding kernels in the spatial domain — and convolution does work — but each one feels ad-hoc and slow. There's a deeper view: every image is secretly a **sum of sinusoidal patterns at different frequencies, orientations, and amplitudes**. If you have access to that decomposition, blurring becomes "delete high frequencies," sharpening becomes "amplify high frequencies," and resizing becomes "delete the frequencies that won't fit." The Fourier transform gives you that decomposition. Sampling theory tells you what frequencies your image can possibly contain given its pixel grid — and what goes wrong when you try to throw pixels away.

## 2. The naive approach and why it's incomplete

You can blur an image by convolving with a Gaussian kernel in the spatial domain. That works — but if the kernel is large, convolution is slow ($O(N^2 \cdot k^2)$ for an $N \times N$ image and $k \times k$ kernel). And it gives you no insight into *what frequencies you're killing*. Convolution-by-hand also doesn't tell you why if you naively downsample (drop every other pixel) you get **aliasing artifacts** — wagon-wheel distortion, jagged edges, false patterns that weren't in the original. Without the frequency-domain view, you can't even diagnose that bug, let alone fix it.

## 3. The key insight (1D first)

**Any signal can be written as a sum of sinusoids of different frequencies.** The Fourier transform tells you, for each frequency $f$, what amplitude and phase of sine wave you'd need to add to reconstruct the signal. That's it. The signal in the spatial domain and its decomposition in the frequency domain are two views of the same object — losslessly convertible.

For images (2D), the sinusoids are **2D plane waves**: patterns of stripes oriented at some angle, with some spacing. A point in the 2D Fourier spectrum at coordinates $(u, v)$ tells you "the image contains a striped pattern with horizontal frequency $u$ and vertical frequency $v$, with this much amplitude." That single point encodes a single global stripe pattern.

## 4. Reading a 2D Fourier spectrum

This is the part you need for **Q7**. Conventions: spectrum coordinates are $(u, v)$, with $u$ horizontal (frequency along $x$) and $v$ vertical (frequency along $y$). A "shifted" spectrum has the **DC component (frequency 0,0) at the centre** of the image, with frequency increasing outward.

Some atlas patterns:

| Image content | Where energy lives in the shifted spectrum |
|---|---|
| Constant grey (no variation) | A single bright spot at the centre (DC only) |
| **Horizontal stripes** (brightness varies vertically) | Bright spots **along the vertical axis through the centre** — because the variation is in $y$, so $v \ne 0$, $u = 0$ |
| **Vertical stripes** (brightness varies horizontally) | Bright spots along the horizontal axis through the centre — variation in $x$, so $u \ne 0$, $v = 0$ |
| Stripes at 45° | Bright spots along the 45° diagonal through the centre |
| Smooth gradient | Energy concentrated near the centre (mostly low frequencies) |
| Sharp edges, fine texture, noise | Energy spread far from the centre (high frequencies) |

The rule: **the orientation of the stripes in the image and the direction from centre to the bright spot in the spectrum are perpendicular.** Horizontal stripes vary in the *vertical* direction → spectrum lights up *vertically*. This trips people up. The frequency axis points along the direction of variation, not along the stripes themselves.

```
Image (horizontal stripes)        Shifted spectrum
█ █ █ █ █ █ █ █                    .       .       .
                                   .       .       .
                                   .   *   .       .
                                   .   *   .       .
                                   .   * DC*       .   ← bright spots on vertical axis
                                   .   *   .       .
                                   .   *   .       .
                                   .       .       .
```

Memorize the perpendicular rule and Q7 is automatic.

## 5. The convolution theorem — the load-bearing fact

**Convolution in space = multiplication in frequency.**

$$f * g \;\longleftrightarrow\; F \cdot G$$

If you blur an image $f$ by convolving with a Gaussian $g$ in the spatial domain, you can equivalently:

1. Take the Fourier transform of $f$ to get $F$.
2. Take the Fourier transform of $g$ (a Gaussian → still a Gaussian in frequency space).
3. Multiply them pointwise: $F \cdot G$.
4. Inverse Fourier transform back to get the blurred image.

This is one of the most useful facts in image processing. It means **filtering is just multiplying a mask onto the spectrum**.

## 6. Filtering by multiplying in frequency space

- **Low-pass filter (LPF)**: multiply the spectrum by a mask that's 1 near the centre (low frequencies) and 0 far from it. This kills high frequencies. Inverse transform → **blurred image**. Loses fine detail and noise.
- **High-pass filter (HPF)**: 1 far from centre, 0 near centre. Kills low frequencies. Inverse transform → **edges and texture**, no smooth regions. This is what edge detection looks like in frequency.
- **Band-pass**: keep only a ring of frequencies. Used for texture analysis at a specific scale.

The shape of the mask matters. A hard circular cutoff causes ringing artifacts (because a circle in frequency space is a sinc function in spatial space — oscillatory). A Gaussian mask gives clean blurring with no ringing.

So: **"blur an image using FT" = LPF in frequency space**. That's half of Q2 right there.

## 7. Sampling and the Nyquist theorem

A digital image is a *sampled* continuous scene — discrete pixels on a grid. Sampling theory asks: when does this sampling lose information?

**Nyquist–Shannon theorem.** To reconstruct a signal that contains frequencies up to $f_\text{max}$ without distortion, you must sample at a rate **at least $2 f_\text{max}$**. Equivalently, to resolve features of size $s$ (one full cycle of variation over distance $s$), you need at least **2 samples per $s$**.

This is **Q25**: to resolve features as small as 1 cm you need ≥ 2 pixels per cm.

**What goes wrong if you under-sample.** Frequencies above $f_\text{sample}/2$ (the *Nyquist frequency*) cannot be represented honestly by the sampling grid. Instead, they get **folded back** into the representable range, masquerading as lower frequencies. This is **aliasing**. A real fence picket at frequency $f$ above Nyquist appears in the sampled image as a lower-frequency pattern that wasn't really there — wagon-wheel effect in video, moiré patterns on textures.

This is **Q16**: violating Nyquist causes high-frequency components to **fold into lower frequencies**.

## 8. Worked example — blur and downsample by 2 (the Q2 answer)

Setup: you have an 8×8 image $I$ and you want a 4×4 thumbnail.

### The wrong way (naive)

Just throw away every other pixel. If the original contains any frequencies above the new Nyquist limit (which is half the original Nyquist, since you halved the sampling rate), they alias into the thumbnail. You get jaggy edges and false moiré patterns.

### The right way using Fourier transform

1. **Compute the FT of the image**: $F = \text{FFT}(I)$. This gives you an 8×8 complex array of frequency coefficients.
2. **Apply a low-pass filter** to kill all frequencies above the new Nyquist. Original Nyquist was at $\pm 4$ cycles/image; new Nyquist (after halving sample rate) is at $\pm 2$ cycles/image. So zero out every coefficient at frequency magnitude $> 2$.
3. **(Alternatively, since you've already filtered, you can crop the spectrum.)** In the shifted spectrum, keep only the central 4×4 region — that's exactly the frequencies that survived the low-pass.
4. **Inverse FFT** the cropped 4×4 spectrum: $I_\text{small} = \text{IFFT}(F_\text{cropped})$. You now have a 4×4 thumbnail with no aliasing.

The key step is **(2) — low-pass first, then sample**. Skipping the LPF causes aliasing; this is *the* mistake that the question is testing.

### Blurring (the second half of Q2)

To blur, the same machinery: take FFT, multiply by a Gaussian mask centred on the DC (a low-pass that smoothly attenuates higher frequencies rather than hard-cutting), inverse FFT. Wider Gaussian in frequency space → narrower Gaussian blur in spatial space, and vice versa (Gaussians are inverses of each other under FT, with inversely-related widths).

### How to write Q2 (template answer)

> *"Both blurring and downsampling are low-pass operations in the Fourier domain.*
>
> *To blur: take the 2D Fourier transform of the image, multiply pointwise by a low-pass filter (e.g. a Gaussian centred on DC) in frequency space — this attenuates high-frequency components which correspond to fine detail and noise — then take the inverse Fourier transform. By the convolution theorem, this is equivalent to spatial convolution with the inverse-transform of the filter, but cleaner and faster (FFT is $O(N \log N)$).*
>
> *To downsample by 2: simply discarding every other pixel causes aliasing (Nyquist), because frequencies above the new sampling rate's Nyquist limit fold into lower frequencies. The correct procedure is (1) take the FFT, (2) apply a low-pass filter that zeroes all frequencies above the new Nyquist (half the original), (3) take the inverse FFT, then (4) sample every second pixel. Equivalently, in frequency space: keep only the central quarter of the shifted spectrum and inverse-transform that smaller spectrum — this is anti-aliased downsampling."*

That answer hits every point and is directly mappable to a 0.75-pt rubric.

## 9. FFT vs DFT (Q11)

Same transform, different algorithms.

- **DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform)**: the brute-force computation. $N$ output samples each computed as a sum over $N$ inputs → $O(N^2)$.
- **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)**: a divide-and-conquer recursion (Cooley–Tukey) that exploits symmetries of the complex exponentials. Runs in $O(N \log N)$.

For an 8-pixel signal: DFT = 64 ops, FFT ≈ 24 ops. For a 1024-pixel row: DFT ≈ 1M ops, FFT ≈ 10K ops. **For images, FFT makes Fourier methods practical.** The transform output is identical — only the algorithm differs.

## 10. The eight facts you need for the exam

- **Fourier transform = decomposition into sinusoids.** Every image is a sum of 2D plane waves at various frequencies, orientations, and amplitudes. The FT gives you the recipe (magnitude + phase per frequency).
- **2D spectrum reading rule:** stripes in the image and the bright-spot direction in the shifted spectrum are **perpendicular**. Horizontal stripes → vertical line of bright spots through DC. (Q7)
- **Convolution theorem:** convolution in space ↔ multiplication in frequency. Filtering = multiplying a mask onto the spectrum.
- **Low-pass = blur**, high-pass = edges/sharpen, band-pass = texture at one scale. Gaussian masks avoid ringing artifacts.
- **Nyquist:** to resolve features at scale $s$ you need ≥ $2/s$ samples per unit. To resolve 1 cm features → ≥ 2 pixels/cm. (Q25)
- **Aliasing:** under-sampling causes high frequencies to **fold into lower frequencies**, producing false patterns (wagon wheel, moiré). (Q16)
- **Correct downsampling = LPF first, then sample.** Always low-pass to the new Nyquist before discarding pixels. (Q2)
- **FFT runs in $O(N \log N)$**, the DFT it computes is the same but takes $O(N^2)$ brute force. FFT makes images tractable. (Q11)

## How to read them (not what)

Don't passively read. For each concept:

1. **Cover the page**, write what you remember the concept *is* in one sentence.
2. **Uncover and compare.** Mark gaps.
3. **Re-cover. Write the key equation or rule from memory.** For Fourier this is convolution theorem + Nyquist. For attention this is the softmax(QKᵀ/√d)V formula.
4. Move on once you can produce both from a blank page.

Two passive readings of a note ≈ one active recall pass. The active pass is what builds exam-grade recall.

Start with Fourier Tier 1 (5 notes). Once those pass the recall test, do my three check questions (F1–F3) on paper. Then move to attention.
